51: en de to sympleroysthai tas hemeras tes analepseos autou — when the days for his assumption were being fulfilled, he set his face to go to Jerusalem — analempsis (assumption, taking up) is a rare word used for Elijah's being taken up (2 Kings 2:11 in Josephus) and for Jesus's ascension (Acts 1:11). Luke frames the Jerusalem journey (Luke 9:51-19:44) as a journey toward the assumption-through-death-resurrection-ascension; Jerusalem is the city where prophets die (Luke 13:33) and where the mission is fulfilled. Estersen to prosopon autou (set his face) echoes Isaiah 50:7 (the Servant who set his face like flint).
1: dunamin kai exousian — power and authority — Luke uses both terms for what the Twelve receive: dunamis (inherent power, capability) and exousia (delegated authority, the right to exercise power). The distinction matters: dunamis is operative force (used for miracles throughout Luke-Acts), while exousia is the authorization that legitimizes its use. Together they signal that the disciples' mission is a genuine extension of Jesus's own ministry, not a lesser imitation. Epi panta ta daimonia — over all the demons — the panta (all) is emphatic: no category of demonic opposition is exempt from the authority granted.
2: kēryssein tēn basileian tou theou kai iāsthai — to proclaim the kingdom of God and to heal — the mission pairing is proclamation (kēryssō = herald, proclaim) and healing (iāomai = heal, cure). Luke's version of the commissioning omits Mark's explicit exorcism mandate but subsumes it in the authority over all demons (Luke 9:1). Kēryssein is the proclamation-verb of the herald who announces an authoritative message; it is not teaching (didaskō) or persuading (peithō) but announcing with the authority of the one who sent the herald.
3: mēden airein eis tēn hodon — take nothing for the road — the equipment list is the most restrictive in Luke: no staff (rhabdos), no bag (pēra), no bread, no money, no extra tunic. Matthew and Mark permit a staff; Luke does not — reflecting either a different source tradition or a stricter symbolic point about radical dependence on hospitality. The prohibition on taking equipment creates the conditions for the hosts' ministry: the disciples' need is what occasions the reciprocal relationship of proclamation and provision.
4: ekei menete kai ekeithen exerchesthe — remain there and from there depart — the single-house strategy prevents the disciples from "house-shopping" for better lodging, which would be an honor-competitive behavior inconsistent with their mission posture. Menō (remain, stay) is not inertia but committed presence — the proclamation happens through sustained relationship with the hosts, not drive-by announcement.
5: ton koniorton apo tōn podōn hymōn apotinassete — shake off the dust from your feet — the dust-shaking was a Jewish practice performed when returning from Gentile territory, as a sign of separation from Gentile impurity. Applied to a Jewish city that rejects the gospel, it signals that the city has placed itself outside the covenant. Eis marturion ep autous — as a testimony against them — marturion (testimony, witness) makes the gesture legal: it is a formal witness-act, not an emotional gesture of frustration.
6: euangelizomenoi kai therapenontes pantachou — proclaiming the good news and healing everywhere — the imperfect diērchonto (were going through) describes ongoing movement through the villages; pantachou (everywhere) is the geographic scope. The paired participles euangelizomenoi (proclaiming good news) and therapenontes (healing) are the practice-form of the commissioning (Luke 9:2); the disciples do what they were sent to do.
7: dietōrei — was perplexed, at a loss — diaporeō (be thoroughly at a loss, be perplexed) is one of Luke's psychological-state verbs (also Acts 2:12; Luke 10:17); the dia- prefix intensifies the confusion. Herod's perplexity about Jesus's identity introduces the question that will be answered at Caesarea Philippi (Luke 9:20). The options he considers — John raised, Elijah, an ancient prophet — map the current popular categories that Peter's confession will transcend.
8: hōti Ēlias ephanē — that Elijah appeared — ephanē (aorist passive of phainō, appear, be manifested) is Luke's verb for supernatural appearance. The Elijah identification is grounded in Malachi 4:5-6; prophētēs tis tōn archaiōn anestē (one of the ancient prophets rose) uses anistēmi (rise, stand up) — the same verb used for resurrection. The popular speculation that one of the ancient prophets had risen is an implicit resurrection belief that Jesus will claim for himself.
9: Iōannēn egō apekephalisa — John I beheaded — Herod's first-person past tense admission is psychologically vivid: he is the one who knows he executed John and therefore knows John is not the one doing miracles. Tis de estin houtos (but who is this?) is Herod's genuine question — the same question the disciples will answer (Luke 9:20) and that Luke's Gospel is structured to answer. Herod's curiosity (ezētei idein auton = he sought to see him) will be satisfied mockingly at the trial (Luke 23:8-11).
10: hupechorēsen kat idian eis polin kaloumenēn Bēthsaïda — withdrew privately to a city called Bethsaida — hupochōreō (withdraw, retire) is Luke's word for strategic retirement from public activity; it occurs at 5:16 for Jesus's wilderness prayer withdrawal. Bethsaida (Peter and Andrew's hometown, John 1:44) is on the northeastern shore of the lake; the withdrawal is both physical (crossing to the other side) and intentional (private time with the Twelve).
11: edexato autous kai elalei autois peri tēs basileias tou theou — he received them and spoke to them about the kingdom of God — dechomai (receive, welcome) is Luke's hospitality verb; Jesus receives the crowd that followed despite the withdrawal. Tous echontas chreian therapeias iāto — those who had need of healing he healed — the imperfect iāto describes ongoing healing activity throughout the day; the healings continue even in the context of kingdom teaching.
12: hē de hēmera ērxato klinein — the day began to decline — the poetic phrase hēmera klinein (day declining, literally "bending/inclining") is Lukan literary style at its most classical; it evokes the end of the day with a participial image rather than a simple time-reference. The disciples' concern for food and lodging is practical; their suggestion to dismiss the crowd uses apolys (release/dismiss) — the same word later used for Pilate releasing Jesus.
13: ei mēti poreuthentes hēmeis agorasōmen brōmata — unless we ourselves go and buy food — the disciples' suggestion involves personal effort and market purchase (agorazō = buy in the market); Jesus's response redirects from market dependence to miraculous provision. Plēn ē artoi pente kai ichthyes duo — five loaves and two fish — the contrast between the crowd's size (five thousand men, Luke 9:14) and the provision (five loaves, two fish) is the setup for the miracle's demonstration of divine superabundance.
14: kataklinate autous klisiās hōsei ana pentēkonta — make them recline in groups of about fifty — kataklinate (make lie down, make recline) is the reclining-at-table position for a formal meal; the feeding miracle is structured as a meal, not a distribution of emergency rations. Klisias (groups, literally "leaning-positions" from klinō) — the same root as the day-declining verb (Luke 9:12). The organization into groups of fifty reflects the Exodus wilderness camp structure (Exodus 18:21; Numbers 31:14).
15: kai epoiēsan houtōs — and they did so — the disciples' compliance without protest or explanation is the narrative signal of faith in action: they organize five thousand people for a meal with five loaves and two fish, trusting the one who gave the command. Luke's terse narration of the compliance focuses the reader's attention on the action Jesus will take rather than the disciples' inner state.
16: labōn de tous pente artous kai tous duo ichthyas — taking the five loaves and the two fish — the fourfold eucharistic action: labōn (taking), anablepas eis ton ouranon (looking up to heaven), eulogēsen autous (blessed them), kateklasen (broke), edidou (was giving — imperfect, indicating repeated distribution). The fourfold pattern is identical to the Last Supper (Luke 22:19) and the post-resurrection meal at Emmaus (Luke 24:30). Luke presents every feeding from Jesus as eucharistically patterned.
17: ephagon kai echortasthēsan pantes — all ate and were satisfied — chortazō (satisfy, fill to capacity — from chortos = fodder, grass, the satisfaction of animals fully fed) signals complete fulfillment of hunger, not partial provision. Artō klasmatōn dōdeka kophinoi — twelve baskets of fragments — the twelve baskets exceed the original supply by any measure; the abundance that remains after the messianic feeding exceeds what was present before. Twelve echoes the twelve tribes and the twelve apostles.
18: en tō einai auton proseuchomenon kata monas — while he was praying alone — Luke introduces the Petrine confession scene with the prayer-notice that is his distinctive signature for major revelatory moments (Luke 3:21 — baptism; Luke 6:12 — choosing the Twelve; Luke 9:28-29 — Transfiguration; Luke 22:41 — Gethsemane). The revelation of Jesus's identity to the disciples comes while he prays; the disclosure is embedded in the filial communion of the Son with the Father. Sunēsan autō hoi mathētai — his disciples were with him — the juxtaposition of his private prayer (kata monas) and the disciples' presence is characteristic Lukan paradox.
19: Iōannēn ton baptistēn, alloi de Ēlian, alloi de hoti prophētēs tis tōn archaiōn anestē — John the Baptist, others Elijah, others that one of the ancient prophets rose — the three popular identifications are the same as Herod's (Luke 9:7-8), showing that the question is widespread. The use of anistēmi (rise up, resurrect) for the ancient prophet — the same verb for resurrection — shows that popular belief in prophetic return already had a resurrection dimension before the Petrine confession redefines the category.
20: Christon tou theou — the Christ of God — Luke's form of Peter's confession is unique: ton Christon tou theou (the Christ of God / the Messiah of God) rather than Mark's ho Christos (the Christ) or Matthew's ho Christos ho huios tou theou tou zōntos (the Christ, the Son of the living God). The genitive tou theou (of God) in Luke's version places the messianic title explicitly within the divine relationship — he is not merely the Messiah of Israel's expectation but the one specifically designated by God.
21: parangeilas autois mēdeni legein touto — commanding them to tell no one — parangelō (command with authority) is stronger than prostassō or keleuō; it is the military command-verb. The Messianic Secret command in Luke is characteristically terse — one verse — before moving immediately to the passion prediction and the cross-bearing call. The confession of messiahship must be interpreted through the cross before it can be proclaimed; the secret protects the confession from premature or misunderstood proclamation.
22: dei ton huion tou anthrōpou polla pathein — the Son of Man must suffer many things — the dei (must, it is necessary) governs the entire passion prediction; it is the divine necessity that runs through Luke's Gospel (Luke 2:49; Luke 4:43; Luke 13:33; Luke 17:25; Luke 24:7, 26, 44). apodokimasthēnai — be rejected — apodokimazō is the technical term for official rejection after examination (testing a coin and declaring it counterfeit); the elders, chief priests, and scribes will examine and formally declare Jesus unacceptable. apoktanthēnai kai tē tritē hēmera egerthēnai — killed and on the third day raised — both verbs are aorist passive infinitives governed by dei; the resurrection is as necessary as the death.
23: arato ton stauron autou kath hēmeran — take up his cross daily — Luke's unique addition of kath hēmeran (daily, day by day) transforms the cross-bearing from a one-time eschatological act to an ongoing daily practice. Mark and Matthew say simply "take up his cross"; Luke's kath hēmeran addresses the ongoing life of discipleship rather than only a final crisis. The daily cross is not a metaphor for general difficulty but the daily pattern of self-denial shaped by the master's self-giving. Arnēsasthō heauton — deny himself — arneomai (deny, renounce, disown) is the same verb used for Peter's denial of Jesus (Luke 22:34, 57); self-denial is the opposite movement to Peter's defensive self-assertion.
24: hos gar an thelē tēn psychēn autou sōsai apolesei autēn — whoever wishes to save his life will lose it — the psychē (life, soul, self) is the subject of the saving/losing paradox. The sōzō/apollymi (save/lose) antithesis uses the salvation and destruction vocabulary throughout Luke-Acts; the word sōzō will be used for healing, rescue, and ultimate salvation interchangeably. Heneken emou — for my sake — Luke lacks the additional "and the gospel's sake" of Mark 8:35; the Lukan focus is on personal attachment to Jesus rather than a program.
25: kerdēsas ton kosmon holon heauton de apolesas ē zēmiōtheis — having gained the whole world but losing or forfeiting himself — the kerdainō/zēmioō (gain/forfeit) pair uses commercial profit-loss vocabulary. Zēmioō (forfeit, suffer loss — a financial term for fining or penalizing) is the word Paul uses for the losses he counted as skubalon (refuse) compared to knowing Christ (Philippians 3:8). The whole world (ton kosmon holon) as the maximum gain is set against the self (heauton) as the thing forfeited — the comparison exposes the asymmetry: the self is worth more than everything else combined.
26: hos gar an epaischunthē me kai tous emous logous — whoever is ashamed of me and my words — epaischunomai (be ashamed of, be disgraced by) is the honor-shame vocabulary of Mediterranean culture; shame is the social emotion of fearing others' judgment. Touto ho huios tou anthrōpou epaischunthēsetai — the Son of Man will be ashamed of him — the future reversal: the social shame of confessing Jesus now is repaid in cosmic shame before the divine tribunal then. Hotan elthē en tē doxē autou — when he comes in his glory — Luke adds the Father's glory and the holy angels alongside the Son's own glory.
27: eōs an idōsin tēn basileian tou theou — until they see the kingdom of God — the saying that some standing there will not taste death until they see the kingdom is Lukan without the parallel Markan qualifier "coming with power." Luke's version may point to Pentecost (Acts 2) as the kingdom's inaugural arrival in power, visible to those present at the conversation. Alēthōs legō hymin — truly I say to you — the amēn legō formula replaced by alēthōs legō is characteristic of Luke's translation of Semitic idiom into Greek idiom.
28: meta tous logous toutous hōsei hēmerai oktō — about eight days after these sayings — Luke's hōsei hēmerai oktō (about eight days) differs from Mark and Matthew's "six days." Various harmonization proposals exist; the most likely is that Luke counts inclusively (including the day of the saying and the day of the Transfiguration). The "about" (hōsei) softens the number. Anebē eis to oros proseuxasthai — he went up on the mountain to pray — the mountain is Luke's prayer setting for major revelations; the Transfiguration occurs in the context of the Son's prayer.
29: to eidos tou prosōpou autou heteron kai ho himatismos autou leukos exastraptōn — the appearance of his face became different and his clothing white, flashing like lightning — Luke's Transfiguration description uses eidos (form, appearance — not morphē) and heteron (different, other — not allos = another of the same kind); the face's eidos became heteron (a qualitatively different thing). The clothing is exastraptōn (flashing, shining like lightning — from exastraptō, a uniquely Lukan hapax), vivid meteorological light imagery. Mark uses stilbō (glitter, gleam); Matthew lampō (shine); Luke's exastraptōn is the most dramatic.
30: Mōysēs kai Ēlias — Moses and Elijah — the two figures who appear represent the Law (Mōysēs = Torah) and the Prophets (Ēlias = the prophetic corpus); their presence confirms that what Jesus is about to do in Jerusalem is the fulfillment of the entire OT witness. Both figures also experienced theophanies on Sinai/Horeb (Exod 33-34; 1 Kgs 19) and both departed from life in unusual ways (Deuteronomy 34:6; 2 Kings 2:11). En doxē — in glory — Luke notes that Moses and Elijah appear in glory (doxa) — they share the transformed state that Jesus is manifesting.
31: tēn exodon autou hēn emellen plēroun en Ierousalēm — his departure which he was about to fulfill in Jerusalem — exodos is Luke's unique term for the subject of the Transfiguration conversation (absent from Mark and Matthew). The word carries enormous theological weight: (1) literal sense = departure, going out (the death-and-resurrection complex as a going out from this life); (2) Exodus allusion = Jesus's death as the new Exodus that fulfills Moses's foundational event; (3) plēroō (fulfill) confirms that the exodos is the completion of what was promised. The Transfiguration is a conversation about the cross framed as the fulfillment of the Exodus.
32: hoi de Petros kai hoi sun autō ēsan bebarēmenoi hypnō — Peter and those with him were weighed down with sleep — bebarēmenoi hypnō (weighed down with sleep — perfect passive participle of bareō = weigh down) foreshadows the Gethsemane scene (Luke 22:45) where they again fall asleep from grief (apo tēs lupēs). Diagrēgorēsantes de — remaining fully awake — diagrēgoreō (remain awake, be fully awake — intensive compound with dia-) describes the state of watchful wakefulness they achieve before the vision ends.
33: epistata epistata — Master, Master — Luke uses epistata (master, overseer — the one standing over) rather than Mark's Rabbi or Matthew's kyrie for Peter's address; epistata occurs six times in Luke and nowhere else in the NT. The doubled address (epistata epistata) signals agitation and urgency. Skenās treis — three tents/booths — skēnē (tent, booth, tabernacle) connects to the Feast of Booths (Sukkot, the tabernacle festival) and to the tabernacle of the divine presence — Peter's instinct to build booths may reflect his sense that the eschatological Sukkot has arrived.
34: nephelē kai epeskiazen autous — a cloud came and was overshadowing them — episkiazō (overshadow, cast a shadow over) is the LXX word for the Shekinah cloud covering the tabernacle (Exodus 40:35). Luke uses the same word for the Spirit's overshadowing at the annunciation (Luke 1:35). The cloud is the divine presence-vehicle throughout the wilderness narrative; its appearance at the Transfiguration signals that what is happening is a new Sinai theophany. Ephobeθēsan de en tō eiselthein autous eis tēn nephelēn — they were afraid as they entered the cloud — entering the cloud = entering the divine presence.
35: ho huios mou ho eklelegmenos — my Son, the Chosen/Elect One — Luke's Transfiguration voice is unique: eklelegmenos (perfect passive participle of eklegō = choose, select, elect) rather than the agapētos (beloved) of Mark and Matthew. The eklelegmenos variant is supported by the Bodmer papyrus (p75), Sinaiticus, and Vaticanus. The "Chosen One" resonates with the Servant of Isaiah 42:1 (eklektos in LXX) and with Luke's elective theology throughout Luke-Acts. autou akouete — listen to him — the imperative akouete echoes Deuteronomy 18:15 (the prophet-like-Moses whom Israel must hear); the command identifies Jesus as the fulfillment of the Mosaic prophet-promise.
36: esigēsan kai oudeni apēngeilan en ekeinais tais hēmerais ouden — they were silent and told no one in those days anything — Luke's concluding secrecy notice uses sigaō (be silent, keep silent — the same word for Elizabeth's husband Zechariah struck silent, Luke 1:20; and for the disciples at the triumphal entry, Luke 19:40). The double negative (oudeni... ouden) and the time limitation (en ekeinais tais hēmerais) are both emphatic — total silence, for now. The resurrection will be the moment when silence ends.
37: katelthen apo tou orous — came down from the mountain — the descent from the mountain after a theophany mirrors Moses's descent from Sinai (Exodus 32:15; Luke 34:29). The scene that immediately follows — the helpless disciples unable to cast out a demon from the boy — creates the Transfiguration's ironic frame: the glory on the mountain meets the failure in the valley. The contrast is the narrative argument for the cross: divine glory does not prevent the Son of Man's suffering but leads through it.
38: edeēthēn tōn mathētōn sou hina ekbalōsin auto kai ouk ēdunēthēsan — I begged your disciples to cast it out and they could not — deomai (beg, plead, pray) is the petition verb used for urgent supplication; the father has begged (edeēθēn = aorist of earnest request) and the disciples have failed. Ouk ēdunēθēsan (they were not able — aorist passive of dunamai) echoes the dunamis (power) given to them in Luke 9:1; the power that was given has not been effectively deployed here.
39: pneuma lambanei auton kai exaiphnēs krazei kai sparassei auton — a spirit seizes him and suddenly he cries out and convulses him — lambanō (seize, take hold of) for demonic possession is vivid — the spirit is possessing agent rather than passive presence. Sparassō (convulse, tear) describes violent physical agitation. The symptom description (crying, convulsing, foaming, barely departing) in Luke's account is among the most detailed in the Synoptics, possibly reflecting Luke's medical interest in symptomatology.
40: kai edeeθēn tōn mathētōn sou hina ekbalōsin — I begged your disciples to cast it out — the repetition of the begging and the failure creates the father's lament: he turned to the disciples as the authorized representatives of Jesus's power (Luke 9:1) and found them unable. The failure sets up Jesus's lament over the faithless generation (Luke 9:41) and then the successful exorcism (Luke 9:42), demonstrating that the disciples' failure was not due to any limit in Jesus's authority but in their own application of it.
41: ō genea apistos kai diestrammenē — O faithless and twisted generation — apistos (faithless, unbelieving) and diestrammenō (twisted, perverted — perfect passive participle of diastrephō = twist, distort) echo Deuteronomy 32:5 (LXX: genea skolia kai diestrammenē = a crooked and twisted generation). Jesus's lament applies the Mosaic wilderness-generation indictment to his contemporaries. Heōs pote esomai pros hymas kai anexomai hymōn — how long shall I be with you and bear with you — the language of divine patience exhausted: anechomai (bear with, endure, tolerate) describes the sustained forbearance of the Son of God in the presence of persistent unbelief.
42: ērrēxen auton to daimonion kai esparagmato — the demon convulsed him and threw him down violently — rhēgnymi (tear, dash, convulse) and sparassō (convulse, tear) describe the demon's violent final resistance before departure. Epetimēsen de Iēsous tō pneumati tō akathartō kai iāsato ton paida — Jesus rebuked the unclean spirit and healed the boy — the exorcism is accomplished with the rebuke alone (epetimēsen = rebuke authoritatively); no extended ritual, no prayer, no struggle. Apedōken auton tō patri autou — he gave him back to his father — the restoration language: the boy is returned to his father, as a healing gift.
43: eplasson de pantes epi tē megaleiōtēti tou theou — all were astonished at the majesty of God — ekplēssō (strike out of one's senses, astonish) with the imperfect describes the crowd's ongoing astonishment. Megaleiōtēs (majesty, magnificence — from megas = great) is a rare word used for divine grandeur (also Acts 19:27 for Artemis's greatness). The crowd's attribution of the exorcism to the majesty of God rather than to Jesus directly is the Lukan pattern: healings and miracles lead to glorifying God.
44: thesthe hymeis eis ta ōta hymōn tous logous toutous — let these words sink into your ears — the idiom tithēmi eis ta ōta (place into the ears) is a Semitic expression for focused, attentive hearing (cf. Exodus 17:14; Job 37:2 — "let my ears take in the vastness"). The command emphasizes the disciples' need to truly receive the passion prediction they are about to hear — not just process it cognitively. ho huios tou anthrōpou mellei paradidosthai eis cheiras anthrōpōn — the Son of Man is about to be handed over into human hands — mellō (be about to, imminently going to) + paradidōmi (hand over, betray) is the second passion prediction in Luke.
45: ēgnooun to rēma touto kai ēn parakekalummenon ap autōn — they did not understand this word and it was hidden from them — the double explanation of non-understanding is unique to Luke: (1) they did not understand (ēgnooun, imperfect of agnoeō = not know); (2) ēn parakekalummenon ap autōn (it was hidden/covered from them — perfect passive participle suggesting a deliberate divine concealment). The passive indicates that the hiddenness is not merely cognitive failure but divine withholding — the disciples cannot understand the passion until the resurrection opens their minds (Luke 24:45). Kai ephobounto erōtēsai auton peri tou rēmatos — they were afraid to ask him about this word — deos (fear/awe) prevents inquiry; the subject is too weighty.
46: eisēlthen de dialogismos en autois — a dispute entered into them — dialogismos (reasoning, debate, dispute) is Luke's word for the internal deliberation that is usually negative — the reasoning of unbelief (Luke 1:29; Luke 2:35; Luke 5:22; Luke 12:17). Here the dispute (to tis an eiē meizōn autōn = who might be the greatest among them) comes immediately after the second passion prediction — the disciples respond to the announcement of the Son of Man's humiliation by debating their own greatness. The irony is sharp and Lukan.
47: ho Iēsous eidōs ton dialogismon tēs kardias autōn — Jesus, knowing the reasoning of their hearts — eidōs (knowing — perfect participle of oida) with ton dialogismon tēs kardias is the omniscience-of-hearts theme that Luke develops throughout (Luke 2:35; Luke 5:22; Luke 6:8; Luke 7:39-40; Luke 9:47). Jesus does not ask; he knows. Epilabomenos paidiou — taking a child — epilambanō (take hold of, grasp) suggests an intentional, deliberate act of drawing the child forward; the child is the object lesson placed in the center of the greatness debate.
48: hos ean dexētai touto to paidion epi tō onomati mou — whoever receives this little child in my name — dechomai (receive, welcome) is Luke's hospitality verb; the child is to be received as a guest, with honor. The chain of receiving: child → Christ → the One who sent Christ → the Father. ho gar mikroteros en pasin hymin hyparchōn houtos estin megas — for the one who is least among all of you, that one is great — mikroteros (comparative of mikros = small, little) as superlative in Koine Greek: "the smallest/least" — the one who occupies the lowest social position in the group is the one who is truly great. The reversal redefines greatness by the criterion of humble service rather than social rank.
49: kōlyomen auton hoti ouk akolouthei meth hēmōn — we forbade him because he does not follow with us — John's report reveals the disciples' proprietary attitude toward exorcistic authority: only those who follow with us (the inner circle) are legitimate. The meth hēmōn (with us) is the exclusive criterion John applies. The debate about greatness (Luke 9:46) and the attempt to control who exercises Jesus's power are two sides of the same status-anxiety.
50: mē kōlyete, hos gar ouk estin kath hymōn hyper hymōn estin — do not forbid him, for the one who is not against you is for you — the criterion is reversed from the disciples' criterion: instead of requiring explicit membership in the following (meth hēmōn = with us), Jesus applies the negative test: whoever is not actively opposing is effectively supporting. The principle is the opposite of the Matthaean version ("whoever is not with me is against me," Matthew 12:30) — both logions express the eschatological urgency of the kingdom, but from different angles.
52: apesteilen aggelous pro prosōpou autou — he sent messengers before his face — apostelloō (send as apostles/messengers) with pro prosōpou autou (before his face) echoes Exodus 23:20 LXX (apostello ton angelon mou pro prosōpou sou — I will send my angel before your face) and Malachi 3:1; the messianic journey is prepared by advance agents just as Israel's wilderness journey was prepared by the divine messenger. The Samaritan mission anticipates the Acts mission (Acts 8).
53: hoti to prosōpon autou ēn poreuomenon eis Ierousalēm — because his face was going toward Jerusalem — Luke's explanation of the Samaritans' refusal: the direction of travel (toward Jerusalem) is the offense. The historic Samaritan-Jewish enmity (cf. John 4:9, 20) made Samaritans unwilling to assist pilgrims headed for the Jerusalem temple, which they regarded as the wrong worship site. The phrase to prosōpon... poreuomenon (his face traveling) personifies the resolve established in Luke 9:51.
54: kyrie, theleis eipōmen pyr katabibnai ap ouranou kai analōsai autous — Lord, do you want us to call down fire from heaven to consume them — the disciples' suggestion cites the Elijah precedent (2 Kings 1:10-12, where Elijah called fire on the captain and fifty soldiers). Hōs kai Ēlias epoiēsen — as Elijah also did (some manuscripts add this phrase). The comparison is theologically revealing: the disciples are thinking in terms of the old prophetic paradigm of judgment; Jesus's response will reframe the mission.
55: strapheis de epetimēsen autois — turning, he rebuked them — strephō (turn) + epitimaō (rebuke) is the same sequence Jesus uses in the exorcisms (Luke 9:42) and in rebuking the fever (Luke 4:39). The disciples' fire-calling suggestion receives the same authoritative rebuke as the unclean spirit — their retaliatory impulse is a spiritual disorder equivalent to demonic opposition. Some manuscripts add: "You do not know what kind of spirit you are of; for the Son of Man did not come to destroy men's lives but to save them" — a textually disputed but thematically accurate expansion.
56: kai eporeuthēsan eis heteran kōmēn — and they went to another village — the terse narrative resolution: no argument, no explanation, simply departure to another village. The pragmatic realism of Jesus's ministry strategy — when rejected, move on — matches the commissioning instruction (Luke 9:5). The Samaritan rejection does not halt the journey; it is absorbed into the Travel Narrative's forward momentum toward Jerusalem.
57: akolouthēsō soi hopou ean apierchē — I will follow you wherever you go — the unconditional offer of discipleship (hopou ean apierchē = wherever you depart) receives the Son of Man saying: foxes have holes and birds have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head. The cost of following is not theoretical piety but actual homelessness and instability. Poreumenōn autōn en tē hodō — as they were going along the road — Luke's Travel Narrative is the frame; these encounters happen on the road to Jerusalem.
58: ho de huios tou anthrōpou ouk echei pou tēn kephalēn klinē — but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head — pou klinē tēn kephalēn (where to lay the head) is idiomatic for having a settled dwelling place. The saying's Christological weight is the title: huios tou anthrōpou (Son of Man — the Danielic figure who receives universal dominion) has no earthly home, no property, no settled shelter. The contrast between the animal world's natural provision and the Son of Man's deliberate homelessness is stark: the one who rules all creation accepts voluntary displacement for the sake of his mission.
59: epitrepson moi prōton apelthonti thapsai ton patera mou — allow me first to go and bury my father — prōton (first) is the problem word: burying the father is the most urgent filial duty in Jewish culture (superseding Torah study for the Rabbis); the candidate is asking for temporary priority of family obligation. Jesus's response ("Let the dead bury their own dead") is one of his most shocking sayings and demands interpretation: either the father has not yet died (the candidate is deferring discipleship until after his natural inheritance) or Jesus is using hyperbole to signal the absolute priority of kingdom over family obligation.
60: aphes tous nekrous thapsai tous heautōn nekrous — let the dead bury their own dead — the paradox deploys nekros (dead) in two senses: the physically dead can be buried by the spiritually dead (those outside the kingdom); those who are alive to the kingdom have an urgent mission that supersedes the burial duty. Su de apelthōn diaggelle tēn basileian tou theou — you, go and proclaim the kingdom of God — diaggellō (proclaim throughout, announce widely) is an intensive compound of angellō; the second candidate is explicitly commanded to the proclamation mission. The call to discipleship supersedes even the most sacred of family obligations.
61: epitrepson moi prōton apotaxasthai tois eis ton oikon mou — allow me first to say farewell to those at home — the third candidate's request echoes Elisha's request to Elijah (1 Kings 19:20: "Let me kiss my father and mother goodbye") — and Elijah granted that request. Jesus denies the equivalent here: the eschatological urgency of the kingdom exceeds even the Elijah-Elisha precedent. Oudeis epibas cheira ep arotron — no one putting a hand to the plow — the plow idiom appears in Luke 9:62's response.
62: oudeis epibas cheira ep arotron kai blepōn eis ta opisō euthetos estin tē basileia tou theou — no one who puts a hand to the plow and looks back is fit for the kingdom of God — arotron (plow) requires eyes forward to plow a straight furrow; looking back (eis ta opisō) produces crooked furrows and wasted effort. Euthetos (fit, suitable, useful) is the practical-effectiveness term — the backward-looking disciple is simply not serviceable for the kingdom task. The image echoes Lot's wife (Genesis 19:26) as the classic OT backward-looking figure; plowing straight furrows requires the same forward commitment as following the eschatological Jesus toward Jerusalem.
